fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out a text templating library — think along the lines of what we scoped out in the Q2 planning doc. The core idea is that devs can pass in a template string plus some structured data and get back rendered output. We want to support the usual stuff: variables, loops, conditionals, and the ability to plug in custom formatting functions. There's also been a lot of pain around reusable template chunks — people keep copy-pasting the same header/footer snippets everywhere, so we need a way to define and include those.

One thing that's come up from the content team is that authors keep accidentally leaving internal notes in the output — we need a way to strip those out cleanly. Also, the data science folks asked about doing simple math directly in the template rather than pre-computing everything upstream.

For error handling, we want something consistent — basically the same normalization approach we used in that parser module from the billing project, where errors get categorized and surfaced in a predictable way rather than blowing up randomly.

We also need a tokenization mode so tooling can inspect templates without actually rendering them. And there's been feedback about inconsistent whitespace around control tags — similar to the trim behavior we discussed for the notification templates last quarter. Make sure the I/O adapter stays cleanly separated from the core logic so we can test the engine in isolation.

Quick follow-up from the questions that came in: for the tokenization mode, the output goes to stdout and it’s one token per line. Literal text chunks and tag chunks each get their own line, and that includes whitespace around tags as its own separate lines too. So for example, ' {{funk}} {{so}} ' becomes ' \n{{funk}}\n \n{{so}}\n '.

On the comment behavior, we’re using block comments with {% comment %} ... {% endcomment %}. Everything inside those tags gets removed from rendered output, but literal text outside the delimiters stays exactly as-is. So 'foo{% comment %} note {% endcomment %}bar' renders as 'foobar'.

For assignment, the form is {% assign varname = expression %}. The expression can be a variable, a literal, or a filtered pipeline, and once assigned the variable is available for the rest of the current render scope. We also do want index access for arrays, so things like {{ foo[0] }}, {{ foo[1] }}, etc. should work.

On conditionals, the if tag needs to handle straight truthiness checks like {% if value %}, boolean operators 'and' and 'or' with short-circuit chaining, and comparisons ==, !=, <, >, <=, >=. If a string contains operator words, like 'foo and bar', that should still be treated as a literal string in comparisons. Nested if/else/endif is in scope, and unless is just the inverse of if.

For custom formatting functions, the registration path is through a filter module/class the engine exposes, for example Template.RegisterFilter. Each filter gets the current pipeline value as its first argument and can also take extra literal or variable args. Chaining uses pipes, like {{ value | filter1 | filter2:'arg' }}. The demo filters we talked about are cite_funny, which prefixes 'LOL: ', and add_smiley, which appends a smiley argument.

Built-in filters should include the text ones escape, truncate:N, truncate_words:N, split, join, replace, strip_newlines, and newline_to_br. For clarity on truncate:N, it cuts to N chars with '...' suffix, so truncate:7 on '1234567890' yields '1234...'. Numeric filters are plus, minus, times, divided_by for integer division, and modulo. Sequence filters are size, first, last, sort, and map.

Also, on error handling, the normalization behavior is exactly the one captured in feature13_error_normalization.json: errors are categorized as 'error=syntax\n' when rethrow_errors=true, or rendered inline as 'Liquid error: <message>' otherwise, and that handling lives in the execution adapter layer. And for whitespace trimming, we do want the dash marker behavior with {%- and -%} as specified in feature12_whitespace_trimming.json, stripping leading and trailing whitespace around control tags, including tabs and newlines.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: Template.Parse(string source) / Template.Render(Hash data, RenderParameters parameters)

trigger_keywords: loop variables available inside for block
forloop index counter
loop metadata like position and length
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Inside a for loop block, the special variable 'forloop' exposes: forloop.index (1-based position), forloop.index0 (0-based), forloop.length (total iteration count), forloop.first (boolean), forloop.last (boolean). Range syntax is (start..end), e.g., (1..3). Loops also support limit:N and offset:N modifiers.

trigger_keywords: partial inclusion syntax
how to include reusable template fragments
include tag with data or collection
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Partials are included with {% include 'partial_name' %}. To pass a single object: {% include 'product' with products[0] %} (the object is exposed under the partial name as the variable). To iterate a collection: {% include 'product' for products %} (renders partial once per item). Local variables can be passed as key-value pairs: {% include 'locale_variables' echo1: 'test123', echo2: 'test321' %}. Partials are supplied via a partials dictionary in the render input.

trigger_keywords: whitespace trimming around control tags
trim markers to remove spaces around tags
how to strip leading trailing whitespace from blocks
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
Whitespace trimming uses dash markers: {%- tag %} trims all whitespace (including newlines and tabs) immediately before the tag in the surrounding literal, and {% tag -%} trims whitespace immediately after the tag. For example, 'foo
	  {%- if true %}hi' renders as 'foo
hi', and '{% if true -%}
hi
{% endif %}' renders as 'hi
'.

trigger_keywords: error normalization categories
how errors are reported to stdout
consistent error output format for parse and render failures
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Errors are normalized into stdout categories. When rethrow_errors is true in the input, a syntax error (e.g., unclosed for block) outputs 'error=syntax\n'. When rethrow_errors is not set, render-time errors such as unknown operators are output inline as 'Liquid error: <message>', e.g., 'Liquid error: Unknown operator =!'. The adapter maps engine exceptions to these normalized string forms.

trigger_keywords: safe member exposure for objects
how to expose object properties to templates
permit specific fields on a custom object
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Custom objects must explicitly expose permitted members to the template engine (e.g., via a Drop or ILiquidizable interface). The member name used in the template (e.g., context.Name) maps to a property on the object (e.g., name). Rendering an object directly ({{context}}) invokes a string representation method (e.g., returning 'ObjectText'). Unexposed members render as empty string.

trigger_keywords: tablerow tag HTML output format
how to render HTML table rows from a collection
table row loop with cols parameter
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The tablerow tag renders HTML table markup: {% tablerow n in collection cols:N %}...{% endtablerow %}. Each row opens with <tr class="rowN"> and a newline, cells are <td class="colN">, rows close with </tr> and a newline. The first row's <tr> is followed immediately by a newline before the first cell. Inside the block, tablerowloop.col gives the 1-based column index within the current row.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the compatibility logic from that login module we did last time
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Safe member exposure pattern from feature14_safe_member_exposure.json: objects must implement a Drop/ILiquidizable interface to expose named properties to the template engine, with a fallback string representation for direct object rendering

context_pointers.vague_reference: reusable template chunks — people keep copy-pasting the same header/footer snippets
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Partial template inclusion via {% include %} tag as specified in feature10_partial_inclusion.json, supporting 'with', 'for', and named local variable passing via the partials dictionary

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail isn't something I can speak to directly — you'll need to dig into the existing codebase or the PRD spec to find the precise behavior. Try grepping for relevant tag names or filter identifiers in the source to find the authoritative implementation.

feature10_partial_inclusion.json.description: Render named partial templates with explicit objects, repeated collections, local variables, nested partials, and dynamically selected partial names.

feature11_html_table_rows.json.description: Render table-row loops as HTML table row and cell fragments with row/column classes, counters, offset, and limit.

input.template: {% tablerow n in numbers cols:3%} {{n}} {% endtablerow %}

cases.expected_output: 1 2 3 4 5 6

input.template: {% tablerow n in numbers cols:2%}{{tablerowloop.col}}{% endtablerow %}

cases.expected_output: 12 12 12

feature12_whitespace_trimming.json.description: Trim leading and trailing whitespace around control tags when trim markers are used.

feature13_error_normalization.json.description: Normalize parse, render, include-recursion, and missing-partial errors into language-neutral stdout categories.

feature14_safe_member_exposure.json.description: Expose explicitly permitted object members and a string transformer while hiding unrenderable objects by default.

feature1_tokenization.json.description: Split template text into literal, output, and block tokens without rendering it.

feature2_literal_and_comment_rendering.j.description: Render plain text exactly and omit comment block contents while preserving surrounding literal text.

input.template: this text should come out of the template without change...

cases.expected_output: this text should come out of the template without change...

feature3_variable_resolution.json.description: Resolve scalar values, nested object paths, and missing values in output expressions.

feature4_custom_filter_pipeline.json.description: Apply caller-supplied text filters, passing the current value and literal or variable arguments through the pipeline.

feature5_builtin_text_filters.json.description: Transform strings and arrays with built-in filters for escaping, truncation, joining, splitting, replacing, and newline handling.

feature6_numeric_and_sequence_filters.js.description: Perform arithmetic, size, first/last, sorting, and property projection operations in rendered expressions.

feature7_conditional_rendering.json.description: Render branches based on truthiness, boolean operators, comparisons, nested conditions, and inverse conditions.

input.template: {% if a or b or c %}YES{% endif %}
{% if a == 'and' and b == 'or' and c == 'foo and bar' and d == 'bar or baz' %}YES{% endif %}
{% if true %}{% if false %}NO{% else %}YES{% endif %}{% else %}NO{% endif %}

feature8_loop_rendering.json.description: Iterate over arrays and ranges, expose loop counters, honor limits and offsets, and support nested loops plus break/continue control.

input.template: {%for i in array limit:4 offset:2 %}{{ i }}{%endfor%}

feature9_assignment_rendering.json.description: Assign values from variables, literals, and filtered expressions so later output expressions can read them.
